coredata.dc:title: Bioinspired carbon quantum dots for sensitive fluorescent detection of vitamin B12 in cell system
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mingqian
Liu, Yajun
Ren, Guohong
Wang, Weigang
Wu, Shishan
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Zwitterion-modification, as a bioinspired strategy, provides greatly promising platforms for biological detection and sensor applications. A green, low-cost and straight-forward method for synthesis of highly fluorescent biomimetic carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) has been developed via pyrolysis of cytidine diphosphate choline (CDPC) and ethylenediamine. The BCQDs with a strong emission at wavelength of 450 nm shows ultrasensitive sensing capability for vitamin B12 with high selectivity. Using the fluorometric assay, the detection limit (DL) for vitamin B12 was found to be as low as 81 nM. Meanwhile, the results of 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo(-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT), hemolysis measuring and morphological characterization of Red blood cells (RBCs) confirms the excellent biocompatibility of BCQDs. The imaging experiments of human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) certify that BCQDs could be served as an effective fluorescent sensing probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of vitamin B12 in biological samples on account of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. The BCQDs, further, were successfully applied to probe vitamin B12 in living cells, which broaden its potential application in vivo system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomimetic CQDs
Vitamin B12
Cell imaging
Biocompatibility
Sensing probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the CDPC-based BCQDs for fluorescence vitamin B12 sensing
Detection of vitamin B12 by biospired BCQDs
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of vitamin B12 to BCQDs
Two-photon 2D and 3D z-stack cell imaging of BCQDs and vitamin B12

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic description of the synthesis of bioinspired BCQDs, detection of Co2+ and vitamin B12, and cellular imaging.

(A) TEM image of BCQDs. Inset is HR-TEM image and particle size distribution of BCQDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of CDPC and BCQDs. (C) Survey spectra of BCQDs. (D) C1s spectrum of BCQDs. (E) Fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra of BCQDs. Inset is BCQDs solution under the illumination of a UV light (365 nm). (F) Excitation dependent emission spectra of the BCQDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of BCQDs with various metal ions. (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of BCQDs in the presence of increasing Co2+ concentrations (0–300 μM). (C) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the vitamin B12 concentration, which is derived from the results in (b). (D) A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of vitamin B12 (0–50 mM).

(A) The proliferation rate of HeLa cells incubated in BCQDs over 48 h with different concentrations of the fluorescent carbon dots. (B) Flow cytometry analysis of HeLa cells after the incubation with 100 μg/mL of BCQDs for 24 h, (a) The control group and (b) BCQDs group. (C) Hemolysis of the RBCs incubated with different concentrations of the BCQDs at room temperature. The changes in cell apoptosis and necrosis are negligible. (D) Morphology and aggregation of the RBCs in the presence of different concentrations of BCQDs (a) negative control, (b) 20 μg/mL, (c) 40 μg/mL, (d) 60 μg/mL, (e) 80 μg/mL, and (f) 100 μg/mL.

3D image of BCQDs and complex which contains BCQDs and vitamin B12 by laser scanning confocal microscope. The pictures from left to right show flourescent images BCQDs (green), (red), overlay of the two bright field images. (A–C) The fluorescence intensity of two-photon imaging with 350 nm pulsed laser: Cell viability of HeLa cells in the presence of BCQDs. Microscopy images; (D–F) further incubated with vitamin B12 for another 1 h at 37 °C. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Bioinspired carbon quantum dots for sensitive fluorescent detection of vitamin B12 in cell system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, 163 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, 210046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, 163 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, 210023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zwitterion-modification, as a bioinspired strategy, provides greatly promising platforms for biological detection and sensor applications. A green, low-cost and straight-forward method for synthesis of highly fluorescent biomimetic carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) has been developed via pyrolysis of cytidine diphosphate choline (CDPC) and ethylenediamine. The BCQDs with a strong emission at wavelength of 450 nm shows ultrasensitive sensing capability for vitamin B12 with high selectivity. Using the fluorometric assay, the detection limit (DL) for vitamin B12 was found to be as low as 81 nM. Meanwhile, the results of 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo(-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT), hemolysis measuring and morphological characterization of Red blood cells (RBCs) confirms the excellent biocompatibility of BCQDs. The imaging experiments of human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) certify that BCQDs could be served as an effective fluorescent sensing probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of vitamin B12 in biological samples on account of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. The BCQDs, further, were successfully applied to probe vitamin B12 in living cells, which broaden its potential application in vivo system.

The biomimetic carbon quantum dots were first synthesized as efficient fluorescence probe for vitamin B12 in cell system. The fluorescence of BCQDs is obviously quenched only by vitamin B12 even at fairly low concentrations. The BCQDs, further, showed its excellent biocompatibility and cell imaging. This result meant that a high selective and sensitive fluorescence probe had been built for the detection of trace vitamin B12.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Learning from nature and being bioinspired, novel biomimetic materials has been developed rapidly in the latest years. Due to their own excellent properties, biomimetic materials are now increasingly being used in biosensors [1–4], imitation protein adhesion [5], nano-drug loading [6,7], bone tissue regeneration [8], and biomimetic catalysts [9,10]. Among them, the applications of biosensors are gaining more and more development due to their simplicity and sensitivity.

Biosensors are used for precise monitoring and detection of different substances and concentrations in the body [11–13]. The surface of biomimetic carbon quantum dots (BCQDs), similar to the structure of the zwitterionic material, contains positive and negative ion groups, respectively, which is compatible with the essential characteristics of the organism [14–17]. Recently, many scientists use biomimetic materials to carry out this focus. Sivashanmugan et al. [18] synthesized a zwitterionic copolymer, namely poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-sulfobetaine methacrylate) (poly(GMA-co-SBMA)), for the surface modification of surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). Riedel et al. [19] used a novel biointerface architecture based on poly [(N-(2-hydroxypropyl) methacrylamide)-co-(carboxybetaine methacrylamide)] brushes functionalized with bioreceptors to analyze clinical serum samples. Gupta et al. [20] synthesized of bright, multicolor fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S@CQDs) has been developed by using simple microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and sodium thiosulphate to detect arsenic and glutathione. It can be seen that the use of biomimetic materials to prepare some of the detection devices can achieve a precise detection of specific substances.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent an emerging class of fluorescent materials and provide a broad application potential in various fields of biological or biomedical applications which contain cell imaging, photoacoustic imaging, photodynamic therapy and targeted drug delivery [21,22]. Traditional CQDs are also used as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive responses of ions and biothiols, such as Hg2+ [23,24], Cu2+ [25,26], Pb2+ [27,28], glutathione [29,30], and alkaline phosphatase [31]. These fluorescent carbon dots probes hold great promise in chemical sensing applications. However, the cells will be damaged when these carbon quantum dots are highly intracellularly enriched. To some extent, it is essential to develop a biocompatible CQDs which can selectively and sensitively detect ions and biothiols.

Vitamin B12 is a complex containing cobalt ions, which is synthesized mainly by microbial and higher flora. And it is the only one vitamins need for the intestinal secretions (endogenous factor) to help to be absorbed [32]. Vitamin B12 is essential for the biological synthesis of molecules such as DNA, and the main physiological function is involved in the manufacture of bone marrow erythrocytes, the arrangement of red blood cells (RBCs) and persistence of the nervous system [33–35]. It can promote the development and maturation of RBCs, so that the body's hematopoietic function in a normal state, the prevention of malignant anemia. Children lack of vitamin B12 early manifestations of mental disorders, expression of sluggish, less cry less trouble, unresponsive, love sleep and other symptoms, and finally cause anemia [36]. Therefore, it is useful to develop highly selectivity and sensitivity analytical methods for the determination of the concentrations of vitamin B12 in the cell system.

In this work, for the first BCQDs were synthesized as an efficient fluorescence probe for vitamin B12 in the cell system. Zwitterionic grafted CQDs were successfully induced by facilely hydrothermal treatment using cytidine diphosphate choline (CDPC) as the precursor, which has affluent -NH2, -PO4 3-, -COOH, and -OH in its structural delivering excellent bionic properties. Without further chemical modification, the sensitive fluorescence quenching of BCQDs by metal ions was studied. The results show that the fluorescence of BCQDs is obviously quenched only by vitamin B12 even at fairly low concentrations. The BCQDs, further, showed its excellent biocompatibility and cell imaging. This result meant that a high selective and sensitive fluorescence probe had been built for the detection of trace vitamin B12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the CDPC-based BCQDs for fluorescence vitamin B12 sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation and sensing mechanism of BCQDs is shown in Fig. 1 . The BCQDs was prepared from CDPC and ethanediamine by a simple hydrothermal process at 160 °C for 10 h. Although a detailed mechanism of the formation process of BCQDs remained unclear, it is proposed that the formation of BCQDs was likely to proceed via two stages with summarizing the previous reports [37,38]. Firstly, the CDPC came to at high temperatures with ethanediamine through the chemical reaction between groups, then the polymerization products by blending and condensation to generate different soluble polymeric products. Secondly, the small dots grew at high temperature with the continuous condensation and finally formed BCQDs nanocrystalline. The surface of lattice structures of BCQDs connected with a vast amount of -OH, -COOH, -PO4 3-, and -NH2 that endowed the BCQDs with charming photoluminescence and excellent bioinspired ability. Moreover, BCQDs solution showed multicolor imaging of green and red under different excitation wavelengths by cultivating with HeLa cells. In addition, BCQDs owned low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Therefore, the as-prepared BCQDs were promising candidates for ion detection and cellular imaging.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, transmission electron microscope (TEM) images showed the size and morphology of BCQDs with an average diameter of 2.84 nm which measured from 50 particles, and all of them were torispherical nanodots, favorably distributed and without agglomeration. The high-resolution-TEM (HRTEM) of BCQDs displayed the presence of amorphous carbon which had well-resolved lattice fringes with an average in-plane lattice spacing of 0.31 nm (Inset of Fig. 2A). Similarly, the X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern of the BCQDs revealed a sharp reflection peak at 2θ = 25.2° (Fig. S1). From Bragg equation (2dsinθ = nλ), the interlayer spacing distance of BCQDs was calculated to be 0.32 nm where d was the interlayer spacing distance of the BCQDs. But, generally, the broad band at this theta can be ascribed to either some amorphous residual organic, which was consistent with many other reports about BCQDs [24,39]. The Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) result indicated that BCQDs possess a narrow particle size distribution with an average hydrodynamic particle size 4.65 nm, as shown in Figure S2A. Contrary to the TEM results, the average sizes of DLS were bigger because it estimated the whole hydrodynamic particle size [40,41]. In the Zeta potential measurement, the value is found near zero, indicating that BCQDs contain large amounts of -NH2 and -PO4 3-, suggesting that they have good water solubility (Fig. S2B).

Furthermore, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were used to analyze some functional groups at the surface of BCQDs. In the FTIR spectrum of BCQDs (Fig. 2B), the wide absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 was a stretching vibration band of O-H. The peak of BCQDs at 1644 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration peak of C=C and C=O, and the peak of 1576 cm−1 was the bending vibration peak of N-H and bending vibration peak of N-H. Additionally, the 500–900 cm−1 absorption peaks were associated with the bending vibration of the C-H which existed in the structure of BCQDs. All of the above indicated that they had a large number of hydrophilic groups (-NH2, -COOH, and -OH) on their surface, and those hydrophilic groups can provide further hydrophilic reactions and cause photoluminescence.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to clarify the surface composition and element analysis of the BCQDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the as-prepared BCQDs are mainly composed of C, O, N, and P with the binding energy peaks at 284.3, 527.3, 397.9, and 132.7 eV. For the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) there were five peaks observed at 284.3, 285.3, and 287.4 eV, which were attributed to the C-C, C-N, C-O, and C=O groups, respectively. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Fig. S3A) showed two peaks at 531.3 and 534.5 eV, which were attributed to C-O and -OH. Fig. S3B displayed the N1s spectrum which could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.5, 399.4, and 400.5 eV, representing N1s states in C-N-C, pyridinic-like N, and pyrrolic-like N, respectively.

The BCQDs have excellent optical properties and examined using UV–Vis absorption spectra and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The BCQDs showed excitation and emission peaks at 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 2E). Similar to other CQDs, showed a sharp absorption peak at 280 nm. The absorption peaks at 275 nm and 340 nm were possibly due to the π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C band [44]. The photographs of BCQDs in aqueous solution displayed bright blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 2E). In addition, the BCQDs showed excitation dependent fluorescence under the excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 600 nm (Fig. 2F). With the excitation wavelength of 300–480 nm, the maximum emission wavelength remained constant. While the excitation wavelength was over 350 nm, the fluorescence emission of the BCQDs was excited dependently. The excitation dependent fluorescence in BCQDs has attributed to several reasons, starting from particle size distribution to the presence of different emissive states and even to sluggish solvent relaxation [42,43]. Specifically, with the increase of the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence peak was red shifted and the peak intensity decreased. The λex-dependent fluorescence behavior was useful for multicolor in vitro biological imaging applications. Such excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior attributed to the existence of a number of surface emissive traps as well as the different morphologies of the materials [44,45]. These chemical structures facilitated the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs from the π and π* electron levels of sp2 clusters, thus either inducing fluorescence or enhancing fluorescence intensity. Based on its FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2B) and the UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 2E), BCQDs emitted blue or longer-wavelength fluorescence, resulting from excited electrons relaxing back to either the sp2 energy levels or defect states by π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C band. Consequently, BCQDs have more defects induced by doping N and P elements onto their surface gained more fluorescence than standard CQDs [46].

As a basic element of the external solution environment, pH played a significant role that affected the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs on the external solution environment. Therefore, the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs was investigated (Fig. S4). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs under the excitation 350 nm did not obviously change when pH was 2–12, which demonstrated that the synthesized BCQDs could apply in wide pH environment. However, when the strong acid or strong alkaline environment, the solution fluorescence will be weakened. At acidic pH, the fluorescence intensity decrease which is contributed -OH in the solution will bind to H+, resulting in a continuous decrease in the amount of -COOH and phosphate groups on the surface of the BCQDs and quenching of the fluorescence. And at alkaline pH, the hydroxides in the solution react with the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the quantum dot, and the fluorescence quenching occurs [47–49]. We found that fluorescence intensity had no obvious decrease under continuous UV irradiation. (Fig. S5). The photostability of the BCQDs was also investigated via exposing the BCQDs suspension to a UV light (365 nm) for 60 h. Meanwhile, the as-prepared BCQDs solution can also keep stable for more than several months in air at room temperature. Interestingly, BCQDs exhibited excellent stability even the NaCl concentration was 1.0 mol/L (Fig. S6), indicating that the prepared BCQDs could work at higher salt concentration. Taking the results above, it was confirmed that the BCQDs had great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

By fitting fluorescent decay curve with a double-exponential function, a fluorescent lifetime of 3.59 ns was calculated for the BCQDs excited at 350 nm (Fig. S7). The fluorescent QY upon excitation at 350 nm was calculated to be 22.3% by using the quinine sulfate as a reference substance. BCQDs have excellent fluorescence properties because BCQDs produced with ethylenediamine gain strong impact of molecular fluorescence in the samples [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of vitamin B12 by biospired BCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the selectivity of BCQDs as a fluorescent probe, the fluorescence intensities of BCQDs were analyzed in the presence of metal ions at a concentration of 200.0 mM (Fig. 3 A). The resulting solutions were incubated for 5 min, and then the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It is found that fluorescence responses of BCQDs are different in the presence of different metal ions even under the same conditions. Impressively, the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs can be greatly quenched in the case of Co2+. Based on this phenomenon, BCQDs were efficiently used for the selective detection of Co2+ in coexistence with other metal ions at a concentration of 200.0 μM (Fig. S8). The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be ascribed to the stronger affinity of Co2+ to -NH2, -PO4 3-, and -COO- modified BCQDs, relative to the affinities of other metal ions. The BCQDs can be used for bioimaging without apparent interference, due to the concentration of Co2+ in the biological system is usually very low.

To discuss the sensitivity of the system, different concentrations of Co2+ from 0 to 300.0 × 10−6 M were added to the aqueous solution of BCQDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured (Fig. 3B). As shown in Fig. 3B, the full emission band is quenched by the presence of increasing Co2+ concentration, implying that adding Co2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of the BCQDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear response with the Co2+ concentration in the range of 0.2–3.0 × 10−6 M in Fig. S9. The Stern-Volmer equation was ΔF/F0 = 0.00867C + 0.00702 (R2 = 0.9949, F0 and ΔF were the fluorescence intensity in the absence and subtract the difference presence of the Co2+, respectively.), the Stern-Volmer quenching constant was 9.25 × 105 L/mol. Under this experiment condition, the limit of detection (LOD) for Co2+ was calculated to be 53 nM according to the equation:
LOD = 3δ/s
Where δ was the standard deviation of the BCQDs blank signals (n = 20, λex = 350 nm) and s was the slope of the calibration plot. This value of LOD was much below the maximum level of Co2+ ions permitted (1.7 × 10−3 M) which the WHO recommends maximum limits of cobalt in drinking water [50]. And it was much lower than those of previously reported carbon dots based nanosensors (Table S1).

For detection of vitamin B12, different amounts of solution were added into BCQDs blank solution. The UV–Vis absorption spectra showed that the BCQDs had an obvious absorption band centered at 340 nm (Fig. S10). In BCQDs solution, vitamin B12 solution displayed a strong absorption centered at 365 nm. The absorption of BCQDs increased evidently upon the addition of 50 μM vitamin B12. In the figure S10, the vitamin B12 shows an absorption shoulder at 360 nm and 500–600 nm, and the intensity of peak has been found to increase on the vitamin B12/BCQDs curve, probably because BCQDs also absorb at 360 nm.

The absorption of the BCQDs increased evidently upon addition of vitamin B12 at a 0.5 μM–300 μM concentration, and the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs solution was gradually quenched. Fig. 3C presented the relationship of the changes in fluorescence intensity with the concentration of vitamin B12. From the Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear response with vitamin B12 concentration in the range of 0.5–3.0 × 10−6 M. The Stern-Volmer equation was ΔF/F0 = 0.0267C + 0.00427 (R2 = 0.9923, F0 and ΔF were the fluorescence intensity in the absence and subtract the difference presence of the vitamin B12, respectively.), the Stern-Volmer quenching constant was 2.87 × 105 L/mol [24]. Under this experiment condition, the limit of detection (LOD) for vitamin B12 was calculated to be 81.0 nM according to the equation. These results indicated that BCQDs can be used as fluorescent nanosensors for ultrasensitive and selective detection of vitamin B12. This fluorescence nanosensor can eliminate the disturbance from the detection medium and provide an idea design strategy for detecting other biological molecules with BCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of vitamin B12 to BCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The main ingredient of Vitamin B12 is Co2+, which is related to the fluorescence quenching of bioinspired BCQDs. The fluorescence of BCQDs was influenced by particle size, electronic transition, complex state, number of surface functional groups [41,43,51]. To elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the BCQDs/Co2+ complex was characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. S11A, the monodispersed BCQDs aggregated seriously in the presence of 200 μM Co2+. In addition, the sizes distribution of the BCQDs before and after addition of Co2+ was determined by DLS. As shown in Fig. S11B, after addition of 200 μM Co2+, the average size of the BCQDs/Co2+ complex was about 900 nm confirming that the aggregation of nanodots was formed.

It is well known that fluorescence emission of most CQDs derives from the radiative recombination of excitations, which can be quenched by metal ions such as Co2+ via nonradiative electron-transfer between CQDs and metal ions. However, it might be a little different for BCQDs. Fig. S13 depicts the physical properties of BCQDs and possible quenching mechanism of BCQDs/Co2+ system. As shown in Fig. S13, it exists some oxygen-containing groups on the surface of BCQDs, which can coordinate with Co2+ to form complexes, leading to the transfer of the electron from BCQDs to Co2+. Interestingly, this electron transfer can be facilitated by the doping of P atoms. N (electronegativity: 3.05) and P (electronegativity: 2.19) atoms are both electron donor, P atoms own smaller electronegativity and larger atomic radius, leading to the valence electrons of P in the third shell tend to lose easily [52] Consequently, the introduction of P into the ball-like BCQDs should effectively tune the electronic local density of BCQDs, which would promote the coordination interaction between oxygen functional groups on the edge of BCQDs and Co2+. In addition, the fluorescence of BCQDs is also closely related to the groups on the surface of the BCQDs. As the surface functional groups of the complex are greatly reduced, the fluorescence of the BCQDs is also gradually reduced. When BCQDs and cobalt ions form complexes, the number of functional groups exposed on the surface of carbon dots is greatly reduced, and the fluorescence intensity of the entire system is also greatly reduced [43].

Meanwhile, to further clarify the mechanism of the Co2+ ion-induced fluorescence quenching of BCQDs, we performed Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations to microscopically understand the interaction of the Co2+ ions with BCQDs (the calculation details are depicted in Supplemental material) [53]. The calculation indicates that BCQDs have the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of −7.18 and −3.88 eV, respectively. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap of 2.68 eV. The binding of Co2+ ions onto the surface of BCQDs can induce the partial transfer of excited electrons from BCQDs to LUMO orbitals of Co2+ ions, causing significant fluorescence quenching of BCQDs/Co2+ system.

Before biological application, the cytotoxicity of BCQDs toward HeLa cell was performed by the conventional MTT assay. The result showed that after 48 h of incubation with 100 μg/mL BCQDs, the cell viability was still over 85% (Fig. 4 A). Because the maximum concentration tested was larger than the necessary quantity and the exposure times were much longer for the bioimaging study, thus BCQDs possess low cytotoxicity and could act as an excellent bioimaging reagent. The BCQDs occupied lots of zwitterions have better biocompatibility.

The cytocompatibility of BCQDs was further determined by the flow cytometry analysis. The results in Fig. 4B illustrated that the rate of cell apoptosis and necrosis is not significantly increased after the incubation of HeLa cells with BCQDs with the concentrations of 100 μg/mL for 24 h. It is proven that the kind of BCQDs synthesized by CDPC have a good cytocompatibility.

Hemolysis is usually considered to be a means of evaluating the biocompatibility of various biomedical materials. Hemolysis causes the release of hemoglobin from RBCs, and indicates the disturbance of RBCs membrane integrity. A hemolysis value of under 5% is regarded as the benchmark of biosafety for biomedical materials [53]. As shown in Fig. 4C, the hemolysis experiments evaluate the blood compatibility of BCQDs with different concentrations solutions. In this case, the results showed that all concentrations with BCQDs possessed favorable blood compatibility because all hemolysis values were less than 5%. The hemolysis rate in the presence of BCQDs is lower than that of the raw material CDPC alone. Therefore, BCQDs possessed better blood compatibility than CDPC thanks to a simple one-step hydrothermal process. The interaction of BCQDs with the RBC membrane played a significant role in vivo application. In this work, the morphology of the RBCs was demonstrated by the optical images from negative control (Fig. 4D,a), positive control (Fig. S14), and 20–100 μg/mL of BCQDs (Fig. 4D,b-f). The morphology of RBCs in the presence of the BCQDs didn't change when compared with negative controls, even at high concentrations of BCQDs. It is inferred that BCQDs possess favorable blood compatibility and negligible toxicity with respect to RBCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-photon 2D and 3D z-stack cell imaging of BCQDs and vitamin B12
ce:section.ce:para: We have evaluated the ability of the sensor to operate in live cells. It was found that the cells incubated with only BCQDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence (Fig. S15B), indicating that the BCQDs could penetrate the cell's membrane and translocate into cells by endocytosis. Whereas those incubated with BCQDs and further with Co2+ showed weak intracellular fluorescence (Fig. S15C), demonstrating that the Co2+ could bind to intracellular BCQDs and quench fluorescence.

Next, to evaluate the potential of BCQDs in 2D and 3D cell imaging, an in vitro bioimaging study using the HeLa cell was conducted. As previously shown in Fig. 2D, BCQDs display excitation wavelength dependent behavior, which allows multi-color fluorescence imaging in biological systems [54]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the green and red cell fluorescence images of BCQDs have excitation wavelength at 488 and 543 nm. The gold cell images are the merged images of green and red. For 2D imaging, the cells were cultivated with BCQDs for 4 h, prior to testing. Images captured using 2D cell cultures (Fig. S16) are unable to confirm whether the fluorescent probes actually penetrated inside the cell or remained on the surface. To resolve this uncertainty, further tests were performed using 3D cell cultures to examine the internalization of BCQDs in the cells. BCQDs was added to the cell culture media at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. From the 3D reconstructs (Fig. 5), we note that BCQDs allows accurate 3D imaging of the HeLa cells and that the BCQDs had penetrated into the cell cytoplasm and the nucleus as shown in Fig. 5 (A–C). Thus, the 3D images confirmed that the BCQDs probes have been internalized by the cells rather than being bound or adsorbed on the surface of HeLa cells.

To further determine the colocalization of BCQDs and vitamin B12 inside the cells, 3D cultured cells with BCQDs were cultivated with vitamin B12 for 5 h and imaged after incubation. From the 3D reconstructions of the cells (Fig. 5D–F), the fluorescence intensity was significantly weakened upon the addition of exogenous vitamin B12 into the BCQDs-pretreated HeLa cells. This indicated that vitamin B12 successfully enters cells and forms complexes in cells with BCQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching of BCQDs. Therefore, this result indicated that the as-synthesized BCQDs possess great potential in serving as optical nanoprobes for intracellular vitamin B12.

In summary, we synthesized the highly photoluminescent BCQDs for the first time using CDPC and ethylenediamine as precursors by a facile, green, hydrothermal method. The prepared BCQDs had a similar particle size and strong fluorescence intensity which was stable at various pH, salt concentrations and UV irradiation environment. Without further chemical modification, the BCQDs were shown to be a highly selective fluorescent sensor for vitamin B12 with a very low detection limit. Furthermore, BCQDs had excellent biocompatibility and could be used as a cell imaging probe and fluorescence sensor for the detection of vitamin B12. The study provides a novel and effective vitamin B12 sensor in biological and environmental samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Supporting information Supporting information
BCQDs cell image BCQDs cell image
BCQDs fluorescence quenching BCQDs fluorescence quenching

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.05.057.
